authentication_mode: existing persisted xAI browser authentication; credential-file presence was verified without reading credential contents
approval_mode: default permission mode with only read_file and list_dir enabled; shell, edit, write, search, web, MCP, and Agent classes are explicitly denied
sandbox_mode: Grok Build has no documented Windows OS sandbox; Test C exposes only read_file and list_dir and does not permit file modification
notes: Grok Build 0.2.101 was installed by the official xAI Windows installer at the explicit user-scoped executable path.

Local --version reported grok 0.2.101 (5bc4b5dfad). Local --help and the bundled xAI headless guide verified --single, --cwd, --disable-web-search, --no-memory, --no-subagents, --tools, --disallowed-tools, --deny, --permission-mode, --max-turns, --output-format, --no-auto-update, and --verbatim.

The current Test C template enables only read_file and list_dir; shell, edit, write, search, web, MCP, and Agent classes are explicitly denied, and subagents are disabled by both flag and environment setting.
Web search is disabled by flag, web fetch is disabled by environment setting, and cross-session memory is disabled by both flag and environment setting.
The generic runner uses the persisted user authentication without reading or copying auth.json.

The first approved Test A attempt established authentication and attributable proxy capture but --max-turns 1 stopped after the reasoning turn before final text; two is the smallest evidence-supported ceiling that can permit the final answer.

Corrected run 20260715T200525528106Z-a-c2be8993 returned OK with exit code 0 and reconciled to CAPTURE_VALIDATED with a valid manifest, complete monitoring, no direct bypass detected, and no tested canary or Git artifact detected.

Test B run 20260715T203244965529Z-b-e1a10968 returned the permitted first line and reconciled to CAPTURE_VALIDATED; the run-specific allowed marker occurred once client-to-server over HTTP and no other tested marker or Git artifact was detected.

The first Test C attempt, run 20260715T224515006132Z-c-4d74d5f0, is preserved as CLIENT_EXECUTION_FAILED: two turns allowed listing and follow-up read requests but no final response. Three is the smallest evidence-supported ceiling that can permit the pending read results to be processed into a response.

Corrected Test C run 20260715T225153120488Z-c-85be9c9a returned a repository summary and reconciled to CAPTURE_VALIDATED; six exact tested markers occurred client-to-server, no Git artifact was validated, and no direct bypass was detected within completed monitoring.
limitations: The installed xAI sandbox guide documents Linux and macOS mechanisms but no Windows enforcement, so no OS sandbox guarantee is claimed.
Grok writes ordinary session records under ~/.grok and local help exposes no no-session-persistence flag; the approved prompt is therefore expected to be stored in the client's own session data.

HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ALL_PROXY, SSL_CERT_FILE, and certificate trust produced attributable decrypted traffic in both approved Test A attempts, but routing and direct-bypass conclusions remain run-specific.
cli-chat-proxy.grok.com is an empirically observed host from the preserved failed first attempt and is a reporting hint, not independent attribution evidence.
Run 20260715T193714004929Z-a-e7f90afd is preserved as CLIENT_EXECUTION_FAILED because its one-turn ceiling produced no final response; it is not a valid no-read baseline result.

Run 20260715T224515006132Z-c-4d74d5f0 is preserved as CLIENT_EXECUTION_FAILED because its two-turn ceiling ended after additional read-only tool requests and before a final repository summary; it is not a valid Test C result.

Windows provides no Grok OS sandbox or CLI path-level read allowlist; Test B's allowed.txt-only restriction was prompt-enforced, and Test C's read_file and list_dir access is not an OS-enforced read boundary. Captured evidence must determine what was transmitted.
No credential, token, account identifier, or auth-file content is stored in this adapter.
